name: IAM Roles and Policies
description: IAM roles for service and cross-account access with identity-based and resource-based policies
key_facts: Identity-based policies attach to users, groups, or roles
Resource-based policies attach directly to resources like S3 buckets
Roles use temporary credentials via STS AssumeRole
Policy evaluation: explicit deny > explicit allow > implicit deny
Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify resources shared externally
common_misconceptions: Resource-based policies can be attached to any AWS resource (only some services support them)
IAM policies are evaluated independently (identity-based and resource-based policies interact)

name: KMS and Encryption
description: AWS Key Management Service for encryption at rest and in transit
key_facts: KMS manages customer master keys (CMKs) for envelope encryption
AWS managed keys vs customer managed keys vs AWS owned keys
Automatic key rotation available (every year for customer managed keys)
S3 supports SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, and SSE-C encryption options
KMS keys are regional; multi-region keys available for cross-region use
common_misconceptions: SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS are the same (SSE-KMS provides audit trail via CloudTrail and key access control)
KMS keys can be used across regions by default (keys are regional, multi-region keys must be explicitly created)

name: Security Groups and NACLs
description: Instance-level and subnet-level firewalls for VPC traffic control
key_facts: Security Groups are stateful: return traffic is automatically allowed
Security Groups support allow rules only, no deny rules
NACLs are stateless: return traffic must be explicitly allowed
NACLs support both allow and deny rules, evaluated in order by rule number
Security Groups applied at ENI level, NACLs at subnet level
common_misconceptions: Security Groups can deny traffic (they can only allow; use NACLs for deny rules)
NACLs are stateful (NACLs are stateless; you must allow ephemeral port ranges for return traffic)

name: WAF and Shield
description: Web application firewall and DDoS protection services
key_facts: WAF protects against common web exploits (SQL injection, XSS) at Layer 7
WAF can be deployed on ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, and AppSync
Shield Standard provides automatic DDoS protection at no extra cost
Shield Advanced provides enhanced DDoS protection with 24/7 DDoS Response Team
Shield Advanced includes cost protection for DDoS-related scaling
common_misconceptions: WAF protects against DDoS attacks at all layers (WAF is Layer 7 only; Shield handles Layer 3/4)
Shield Standard requires a subscription (it is automatically included for all AWS customers)

name: CloudTrail
description: API activity logging service for governance, compliance, and auditing
key_facts: Records API calls made in your AWS account
Management events logged by default (90 days in Event History)
Data events (S3 object-level, Lambda invocations) require explicit configuration
Trails can deliver logs to S3 and CloudWatch Logs
Organization trail can log events for all accounts
common_misconceptions: CloudTrail logs all events by default (data events must be explicitly enabled)
CloudTrail and CloudWatch are the same (CloudTrail logs API calls, CloudWatch monitors metrics and logs)

name: Multi-AZ and High Availability
description: Deploying resources across multiple Availability Zones for fault tolerance
key_facts: Multi-AZ deployments survive AZ failures
RDS Multi-AZ uses synchronous replication with automatic failover
ELB automatically distributes traffic across AZs
Auto Scaling can launch instances in multiple AZs
S3 automatically stores data across at least 3 AZs (except One Zone-IA)
common_misconceptions: Multi-AZ RDS improves read performance (it is for HA only; use Read Replicas for read scaling)
Multi-AZ is the same as multi-region (Multi-AZ is within one region)

name: Auto Scaling
description: Automatic scaling of compute resources based on demand
key_facts: Launch templates define the instance configuration
Scaling policies: target tracking, step scaling, simple scaling, scheduled
Target tracking is the recommended default (e.g., maintain 50% CPU utilization)
Cooldown periods prevent rapid scaling oscillation
Predictive scaling uses ML to pre-scale based on traffic patterns
common_misconceptions: Auto Scaling only scales out (it scales both in and out based on policies)
Auto Scaling works immediately (cooldown periods and health check grace periods cause delays)

name: Elastic Load Balancing
description: Application, Network, and Gateway Load Balancers for traffic distribution
key_facts: ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS), supports path-based and host-based routing
NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP), provides ultra-low latency and static IP
GLB operates at Layer 3, used for deploying third-party virtual appliances
ALB supports target groups with EC2, ECS, Lambda, and IP addresses
Connection draining (deregistration delay) allows in-flight requests to complete
common_misconceptions: ALB can handle millions of requests per second with static IP (NLB provides static IP; use NLB or Global Accelerator for static IPs with ALB)
All load balancers support WebSocket (ALB natively supports WebSocket, NLB supports it at TCP level)

name: Route 53 Routing and Failover
description: DNS routing policies for high availability and disaster recovery
key_facts: Simple routing: single resource, no health checks
Weighted routing: distribute traffic by percentage across resources
Latency-based routing: route to the lowest-latency region
Failover routing: active-passive with automatic failover using health checks
Geolocation routing: route based on user geographic location
common_misconceptions: Failover routing requires manual intervention (it is automatic based on health checks)
Latency routing uses geographic proximity (it measures actual network latency, not distance)

name: S3 Replication
description: Cross-region and same-region replication for S3 objects
key_facts: Cross-Region Replication (CRR) copies objects to a bucket in a different region
Same-Region Replication (SRR) copies objects within the same region
Versioning must be enabled on both source and destination buckets
Replication is asynchronous
Delete markers are not replicated by default
common_misconceptions: Replication copies existing objects automatically (only new objects are replicated; use S3 Batch Replication for existing objects)
Delete operations replicate by default (delete markers are not replicated unless configured)

name: RDS Multi-AZ and Read Replicas
description: Database high availability with Multi-AZ and read scaling with replicas
key_facts: Multi-AZ: synchronous standby in another AZ for automatic failover
Read Replicas: asynchronous replication for read scaling
Read Replicas can be cross-region for disaster recovery
Read Replicas can be promoted to standalone databases
Aurora supports up to 15 replicas with automatic failover and replication lag under 100ms
common_misconceptions: Multi-AZ standby can serve read traffic (only the primary handles traffic; use Read Replicas for reads)
Read Replicas provide automatic failover (only Multi-AZ provides automatic failover in standard RDS)

name: CloudFront
description: Content delivery network for low-latency global content distribution
key_facts: Caches content at 400+ Edge Locations globally
Origins can be S3, ALB, EC2, or any HTTP endpoint
Origin Access Control (OAC) restricts S3 access to CloudFront only
Supports custom SSL certificates and alternate domain names
Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions for edge compute
common_misconceptions: CloudFront only serves static content (it can also accelerate dynamic content and APIs)
Origin Access Identity is the current best practice (OAC has replaced OAI as the recommended approach)

name: ElastiCache
description: Managed in-memory caching with Redis and Memcached
key_facts: Redis supports data persistence, replication, Multi-AZ failover, and pub/sub
Memcached supports multi-threaded architecture and simple caching with no persistence
Common patterns: lazy loading (cache on read miss) and write-through (cache on write)
ElastiCache can store session data to make applications stateless
Redis Global Datastore provides cross-region replication
common_misconceptions: Redis and Memcached have the same features (Redis supports persistence and replication, Memcached does not)
ElastiCache replaces a database (it supplements databases by caching frequently accessed data)

name: DynamoDB
description: Serverless NoSQL database with single-digit millisecond latency
key_facts: Key-value and document data model with partition key and optional sort key
Provisioned mode: specify RCUs and WCUs; On-demand mode: pay per request
DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) provides microsecond latency caching
Global Tables for multi-region active-active replication
DynamoDB Streams capture item-level changes for event-driven architectures
common_misconceptions: DynamoDB is good for complex relational queries with joins (use RDS or Aurora for relational workloads)
On-demand mode is always cheaper (provisioned capacity is cheaper for predictable, steady workloads)

name: Amazon Aurora
description: Cloud-native relational database with high performance and availability
key_facts: Compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL
5x throughput of MySQL, 3x throughput of PostgreSQL
Storage auto-scales up to 128 TB in 10 GB increments
Up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms replication lag
Aurora Serverless v2 scales capacity in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU minimum)
common_misconceptions: Aurora is just a managed MySQL/PostgreSQL (it has a completely different storage architecture with 6-way replication)
Aurora Serverless can scale to zero (v2 has a minimum of 0.5 ACU)

name: EBS Volume Types
description: Block storage volume types optimized for different workloads
key_facts: gp3: general purpose SSD, 3000 IOPS baseline, independently configurable IOPS and throughput
io2 Block Express: provisioned IOPS SSD, up to 256,000 IOPS, for critical databases
st1: throughput-optimized HDD for large sequential workloads like data warehouses
sc1: cold HDD, lowest cost for infrequently accessed data
Only SSD volumes (gp2/gp3, io1/io2) can be boot volumes
common_misconceptions: gp2 and gp3 have the same pricing model (gp3 is cheaper and allows independent IOPS/throughput configuration)
HDD volumes can be used as boot volumes (only SSD volumes can be boot volumes)

name: EFS (Elastic File System)
description: Managed NFS file system for shared access across multiple instances
key_facts: NFS-based shared file system accessible from multiple EC2 instances concurrently
Automatically scales storage capacity as files are added or removed
Supports two performance modes: General Purpose and Max I/O
Storage classes: Standard and Infrequent Access with lifecycle management
Works across AZs within a region; One Zone option for cost savings
common_misconceptions: EFS works with Windows instances (EFS is Linux/NFS only; use FSx for Windows)
EFS has a fixed storage capacity (it auto-scales with no pre-provisioning required)

name: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans
description: Commitment-based pricing models for significant compute cost savings
key_facts: Standard RIs: up to 72% savings, locked to instance type and region
Convertible RIs: up to 66% savings, can change instance family, OS, and tenancy
Compute Savings Plans: flexible across instance families, regions, OS, and compute services
EC2 Instance Savings Plans: locked to instance family in a region, higher discount
1-year or 3-year term; all upfront payment gives maximum discount
common_misconceptions: Reserved Instances reserve physical capacity by default (only Zonal RIs reserve capacity; Regional RIs do not)
Savings Plans and RIs provide the same flexibility (Savings Plans are more flexible, especially Compute Savings Plans)

name: Spot Instances
description: Spare EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount with interruption risk
key_facts: Up to 90% savings compared to On-Demand pricing
AWS can reclaim instances with a 2-minute warning
Ideal for batch processing, CI/CD, big data, stateless web servers
Spot Fleet: collection of Spot and optional On-Demand instances
Can combine with Auto Scaling using mixed instance policies
common_misconceptions: Spot Instances are unreliable for all workloads (they are excellent for fault-tolerant and flexible workloads)
Spot price can exceed On-Demand price (Spot price is capped at On-Demand price)

name: S3 Storage Classes and Lifecycle
description: S3 storage tiers and automated lifecycle transitions for cost optimization
key_facts: Standard: frequent access, 99.99% availability
Standard-IA: infrequent access, lower storage cost but retrieval fee, 30-day minimum
One Zone-IA: single AZ, 20% cheaper than Standard-IA
Glacier Instant Retrieval: millisecond access for quarterly data
Lifecycle rules automate transitions between storage classes and object expiration
common_misconceptions: Objects can be transitioned back from Glacier to Standard via lifecycle rules (transitions are one-directional; restore first then copy)
Intelligent-Tiering has retrieval fees for frequent and infrequent tiers (retrieval fees only apply to archive tiers)

name: Right-Sizing and Cost Tools
description: Optimizing resource sizes and using AWS cost management tools
key_facts: AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization and recommends optimal instance types
Cost Explorer visualizes spending trends and provides Reserved Instance recommendations
AWS Budgets sets alerts when spending exceeds defined thresholds
Trusted Advisor identifies idle resources and underutilized instances
Right-sizing should be done continuously, not just at initial deployment
common_misconceptions: Right-sizing only means downsizing (it can also mean upgrading to a more cost-effective instance family)
Cost optimization is a one-time activity (it should be continuous as workloads and pricing change)
